title: Ion Transport in Glassy Side-Group Liquid Crystalline Polymer Electrolytes
abstract:

832
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
0935-9648/99/1007-0832 $ 17.50+.50/0
Adv. Mater. 1999, 11, No. 10
[37] R. H. Friend, in Conjugated Polymers and Related Materials (Eds:
W. R. Salaneck, I. Lundström, B. Rånby), Oxford University Press,
Oxford 1993, p. 285.
[38] P. L. Burn, D. D. C. Bradly, A. R. Brown, R. H. Friend, D. A. Halli-
day, A. B. Holmes, A. Kraft, J. H. F. Martens, Springer Series in Solid
State Sciences 1992, 107, 293.
Ion Transport in Glassy Side-Group Liquid
Crystalline Polymer Electrolytes**
By Corrie T. Imrie,* Malcolm D. Ingram, and
Gillian S. McHattie
It is generally accepted that ionic transport in typical
polymer electrolytes is highly coupled to segmental mo-
tions of the polymer backbone.[1] This has the effect of se-
verely restricting cation mobility as the glass transition
temperature is approached.[2] We describe here the proper-
ties of a new side-group liquid crystalline polymer electro-
lyte system in which the ionic conductivity is insensitive to
Tg, and sub-Tg conductivities of ca. 10±5 S cm±1 are found.
This new pattern of behavior may depend on the establish-
ment of a particular kind of ultrastructure which in turn in-
hibits the formation of ethylene oxide coils which act as
ªion trapsº. This new approach to polymer design offers
the opportunity of enhanced cation transport in polymer
electrolytes with improved performance in batteries and
other electrochemical applications.
Electrolytes are classified according to their ability to
conduct above and below the glass transition temperature,
Tg. Typically, ªsalt-in-polymerº electrolytes exhibit appre-
ciable conductivities only at temperatures above Tg,[2]
whereas superionic glasses[2±4] and ªpolymer-in-saltº elec-
trolytes[5,6] may show substantial sub-Tg ionic conductivity.
In work spanning two decades[7±9] research in polymer elec-
trolytes has largely focused upon enhancing the flexibility
of the polymer matrix in order to promote ionic mobility.
This has met with success but has had the disadvantage that
the mechanism of conduction is dominated by ion±polymer
interactions and the transport numbers of cations (vital to
battery applications) can be very low.[10,11] Sub-Tg conduc-
tivity in poly(vinyl alcohol) has been reported,[12,13] but
there is no evidence yet that this is part of a more general
phenomenon.
Wright et al.[14,15] have recently shown that it is possible
to introduce ultrastructure into polymer electrolytes via
the introduction of non-conducting side chains. We de-
scribe here the remarkable properties of a new type of
polymer electrolyte based on this design strategy, see
Figure 1.
The
example
described
here
is
denoted
MeOC6G6, and contains 4-methoxybiphenyl side groups
attached by alkyl spacers to the ethylene oxide backbone;
0G6 is the ªparent materialº without side groups. The syn-
thesis of these materials has been described elsewhere.[16]
The polymer is doped with lithium perchlorate to give a
10:1 (AO:Li) stoichiometry, where active oxygen (AO) in-
cludes all oxygen atoms along the backbone with the ex-
ception of carbonyl oxygen atoms. The thermal behavior of
polymers and polymer±salt complexes was determined by
differential scanning calorimetry, and mechanical proper-
ties were evaluated by dynamic mechanical thermal analy-
sis (DMTA) in the shear mode, at a frequency of 1 Hz. The
ionic conductivities were measured using standard ac impe-
dance methods.
Fig. 1. Molecular structures of the new polymer electrolytes: a) MeOC6G6
and b) 0G6.
The polymer MeOC6G6 shows a Tg at 278 K, and exhib-
its a smectic liquid crystal phase, undergoing a transition to
the isotropic phase at 317 K. When viewed through the po-
larized light microscope a rather poorly defined focal conic
texture is obtained from which an unambiguous phase as-
signment could not be made. Prolonged annealing periods
did not result in any marked changes to this texture. The
observation of focal conic defects, however, indicates a
smectic layered structure. The smectic phase of several
members of this polymer series has been studied using X-
ray diffraction (XRD). The smectic periodicity is 44.7 
which corresponds to approximately twice the length of the
side groups implying a bilayer arrangement. In the wide an-
gle region peaks were observed at 4.7 and 3.8 . These are
characteristically found for smectic phases but are also
present in the diffraction pattern of poly(ethylene oxide).
Thus there remains the possibility that these peaks arise
both from the lateral separations of the mesogenic units
within the smectic layers and/or from the ethylene oxide
segments. In addition, there is a peak centered at 12.8 
whose origin is unclear but may be associated with the sep-
aration of the isophthalate groups along the backbone.
The addition of lithium perchlorate increases these tran-
sition temperatures to 293 K and 325 K, respectively, but
does not appear to change the nature of the smectic phase.
Specifically, the optical texture of the phase is unchanged
±
[*] Dr. C. T. Imrie, Prof. M. D. Ingram, Dr. G. S. McHattie
Department of Chemistry, University of Aberdeen
Meston Walk, Old Aberdeen AB24 3UE (UK)
[**] The University of Aberdeen is gratefully acknowledged for financial
support.
 and the periodicity and wide angle scattering are identical.
New peaks are present in the XRD pattern of the complex,
however, at 10.6 and 13.8  while the peak centered at
12.8  in that of the polymer is absent. The molecular sig-
nificance of these differences is unclear.
The change in the heat capacity at Tg for both the poly-
mer and complex is very small. The rise in the Tg on addi-
tion of salt is, however, consistent with the loss of segmen-
tal mobility due to the coordination of lithium ions by
ether oxygen atoms. The accompanying increase in the
smectic±isotropic transition temperature indicates some
coupling between the behavior of mesogenic and ethylene
oxide groups. Presumably, the preferential dissolution of
the ions in the backbone reinforces the microphase separa-
tion driving the formation of the smectic phase. Further-
more, dynamic mechanical thermal analysis reveals that a
sudden decrease in the shear modulus of the polymer±salt
complex occurs not at Tg but at 318 K, which is coincident
with the smectic±isotropic transition as detected both ther-
mally and optically. Below this temperature, the shear
modulus is constant at ca. 108 Pa, a value characteristic of
solids. The apparent failure of DMTA to detect the glass
transition observed using differential scanning calorimetry
(DSC) is at first sight most surprising but is consistent with
similar measurements on a range of side group liquid crys-
tal polymers. The ionic conductivity of the MeOC6G6±Li-
ClO4 complex lies in the range 2 ´ 10±7 to 10±4 S cm±1 over
the temperature range 299 to 381 K. These values of con-
ductivity are typical of, if not somewhat poorer than, those
found for conventional non-plasticized but amorphous
poly(ethylene oxide)-based materials.
The remarkable character of this system is revealed,
however, if we overlay the data from all three techniques
as in Figure 2. It is clear that log(ionic conductivity) does
not decrease dramatically as Tg is approached, but varies
essentially in a linear (Arrhenius) fashion over a tempera-
ture range inclusive of Tg. This behavior is typically that of
a decoupled system, i.e., one in which the ions move inde-
pendently of viscous flow in the host matrix.[9] Indeed ap-
preciable ionic conductivities are measured for this com-
plex in a temperature regime in which its mechanical
properties are entirely solid-like. From the dependence of
the log(ionic conductivity) on the reciprocal temperature
an activation energy of 31 kJ mol±1 may be calculated
which is also evidence of a relatively fast ion transport
mechanism and is considerably smaller than that found for
conventional polymer electrolytes.
The question arises if the liquid crystallinity is at the root
of this remarkable behavior. We have examined the prop-
erties of the amorphous polymer, 0G6, and its amorphous
complex with LiClO4. We found 0G6 has a Tg of 237 K,
while the AO:Li = 10:1 complex has a higher Tg of 262 K.
This increase in Tg can be attributed as before to the inter-
action between the ether oxygen atoms and the lithium
ions which serve to stiffen the backbone. Tg is detected by
DMTA at a still higher value of 283 K, reflecting the higher
measuring frequency. Figure 3 compares the Arrhenius de-
pendence of ionic conductivity with the DSC and DMTA
traces. Now log(ionic conductivity) does indeed decrease as
Tg is approached, but the conductivities in the ªrelaxation
regionº (real shear modulus G¢ ^ 106 Pa) are still higher
than would be expected for a fully coupled system.[9] The
activation energy calculated from the data shown in Fig-
ure 3 is 36 kJ mol±1 and also implies relatively fast ion
transport.
The degree of decoupling in amorphous electrolytes is
usually quantified within the framework proposed by An-
gell,[2,17] based on earlier work by Moynihan and co-work-
ers.[18] Thus, the decoupling index, Rt, at Tg can be defined
as, Rt = ts / ts, where ts is the shear relaxation time and ts
is the conductivity relaxation time. Bearing in mind the
structural complexity of liquid-crystalline materials such an
approach may not be appropriate here. Useful insight is
gained ªvisuallyº by comparing Figures 2 and 3. Only in
the side-group liquid-crystal polymer electrolyte system,
Figure 2, does a sharp distinction emerge between continu-
ous variations in conductivity and discontinuous thermal
and mechanical properties.
The most tempting rationalization of the extraordinary
properties of the liquid crystal complex invokes a high de-
gree of decoupling between the mesogenic side groups and
the backbone which results in a phase separated morphol-
ogy in which one domain is associated with the side chains
and the other with the backbones. Thus, the polymer back-
bone would remain mobile at temperatures below the Tg of
the side chains and a highly coupled conventional conduc-
tion mechanism would still operate. Such an explanation is
not without appeal but we would argue it is unlikely to be
correct. The phases exhibited by the MeOC6G6±LiClO4
complex are homogeneous, and just one glass transition can

lus) (*), and heat flow (solid line) on reciprocal temperature for the 10:1
AO:Li MeOC6G6 complex.

The most tempting rationalization of the extraordinary
properties of the liquid crystal complex invokes a high de-
gree of decoupling between the mesogenic side groups and
the backbone which results in a phase separated morphol-
ogy in which one domain is associated with the side chains
and the other with the backbones. Thus, the polymer back-
bone would remain mobile at temperatures below the Tg of
the side chains and a highly coupled conventional conduc-
tion mechanism would still operate. Such an explanation is
not without appeal but we would argue it is unlikely to be
correct. The phases exhibited by the MeOC6G6±LiClO4
complex are homogeneous, and just one glass transition can

Fig. 2. Dependence of the log(ionic conductivity) (&), log(real shear modu-
lus) (*), and heat flow (solid line) on reciprocal temperature for the 10:1
Ó WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
0935-9648/99/1007-0834 $ 17.50+.50/0
be detected. Furthermore, the mechanical properties of the
complex are solid-like at temperatures below the observ-
able glass transition, which would not be the case for a phase
separated material containing rubbery microdomains.
Instead, we advance an explanation for the extraordinary
properties of this liquid crystal complex by invoking a high
degree of coupling between mesogenic side groups and the
ethylene oxide backbone which thereby inhibits the forma-
tion of helical conformations favored by ªfreeº poly(ethyl-
ene oxide) chains. Within such helical arrangements the
lithium ions would be tightly coordinated below Tg and ef-
fectively trapped. By suppressing the formation of such he-
lical structures, an open ethylene oxide structure is ob-
tained within which lithium ions are free to move and
where empty sites exist for the ions to occupy. It is remark-
able that a similar (but weaker) effect is observed in the
amorphous material. It seems that insertion of rigid iso-
phthalate units also inhibits helical formation, sufficient to
provide a measure of ionic decoupling, but for the liquid
crystal complex the open structure is further stabilized via
the interactions between the liquid crystal side groups. It is
worth noting that this view is supported by the continuity
of behavior from the melt into the glassy state in both the
heat capacity and electrical conductivity, indicating that the
structure of the melt is not strongly influenced by tempera-
ture. In addition, the dissolution of the ions in the back-
bone does not swell the smectic layer and thus, the open
network must be relatively unchanged at least in the direc-
tion normal to the smectic layers. This suggests that by
careful engineering of the types of liquid crystal phase pres-
ent, it should be possible to tailor the conductivity mecha-
nism to particular applications.
It is also remarkable that the conductivity in the
MeOC6G6 complex increases strongly (by several orders
of magnitude) as the AO:Li ratio is decreased from 10:1 to
3:1. We should now be able to employ more concentrated
polymer electrolytes than is presently possible with conven-
tional materials. Transport number data for these electro-
lytes are not yet available, but it is tempting to speculate
that the elimination of ªcation trappingº within the ethyl-
ene oxide helix will lead to substantial increases in cation
mobility. For many years this has been one of the principal
goals of polymer electrolyte research.
Final version: February 22, 1999

Dissolution of Single-Walled Carbon
By Mark A. Hamon, Jian Chen, Hui Hu, Yongsheng Chen,
Misha E. Itkis, Apparao M. Rao, Peter C. Eklund,
The scientific and technological potential of single-
walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) has attracted consider-
able attention to this field. Previous studies have been car-
ried out on solid, intractable forms of SWNTs.[1±6] We have
recently shown that end-group functionalization of short
(100±300 nm in length)[5] SWNTs allows the preparation of
stable organic solutions of these materials.[7] The prep-

lus) (*), and heat flow (solid line) on reciprocal temperature for the 10:1
